mechanism_narrative: Sustained growth in non-farm payrolls (PAYEMS) indicates a strengthening labor market and broader economic recovery. This improvement in employment boosts consumer confidence and corporate earnings expectations, leading to positive S&P 500 forward returns as investors price in future growth.
failure_condition: SPX produces negative 20d returns in > 60% of periods where PAYEMS shows consistent month-over-month growth, or the correlation between PAYEMS growth and SPX returns is not statistically significant.

mechanism_narrative: When the federal funds rate (FEDFUNDS) is at or near the zero lower bound, it signals an extremely accommodative monetary policy stance. This low cost of capital encourages investment and risk-taking, compressing discount rates for equities and driving positive S&P 500 forward returns over a longer horizon.
failure_condition: SPX 60-day returns are consistently negative or flat during periods when FEDFUNDS remains below 0.25% for at least three consecutive months.

mechanism_narrative: Robust growth in retail sales (RSAFS) combined with a declining unemployment rate (UNRATE) indicates strong consumer demand and a healthy labor market. This dual signal suggests increasing household income and spending, which directly translates to higher corporate revenues and profits, leading to positive S&P 500 returns.
candidate_trigger: RSAFS 3-month average growth > 0.5% AND UNRATE 3-month average change < -0.1%
failure_condition: SPX 20-day returns are negative or flat in more than 50% of instances where both retail sales growth and unemployment decline conditions are met.

mechanism_narrative: During periods of subdued industrial activity, a significant month-over-month increase in industrial production (INDPRO) signals a potential turning point or acceleration in the manufacturing sector. This improvement in supply-side activity boosts corporate earnings expectations for industrial firms, driving short-term positive S&P 500 returns.
failure_condition: SPX 5-day returns are negative following more than 70% of INDPRO upticks during periods when INDPRO is below its 5-year average.

mechanism_narrative: A persistently high unemployment rate (UNRATE) signals structural weakness in the labor market, leading to reduced consumer spending and heightened economic uncertainty. This environment negatively impacts corporate profitability and investor sentiment, resulting in negative S&P 500 forward returns.
failure_condition: SPX produces positive 20d returns in > 60% of triggered instances despite the unemployment rate remaining above 8.0% for at least three consecutive months.

mechanism_narrative: Consistent growth in housing starts (HOUST) reflects increasing construction activity, investment, and consumer confidence in the housing market. This expansion in a key economic sector generally correlates with broader economic health and positive equity market performance over a longer horizon.
failure_condition: SPX 60-day returns are not positively correlated with HOUST month-over-month growth over any 5-year rolling window, or the correlation is statistically insignificant.

mechanism_narrative: High consumer price inflation (CPIAUCSL) combined with very low federal funds rates (FEDFUNDS) creates a negative real interest rate environment. This encourages investors to seek higher returns in real assets and equities as a hedge against inflation, driving positive S&P 500 forward returns.
failure_condition: SPX fails to generate positive 20-day returns in more than 50% of periods where inflation is high and policy rates are low, as defined by the trigger.

mechanism_narrative: During periods of economic contraction or slow growth, a significant acceleration in real GDP (GDPC1) growth signals a robust recovery. This strong economic momentum translates into improved corporate earnings and investor optimism, leading to positive S&P 500 forward returns.
failure_condition: SPX 60-day returns are negative or flat following periods of strong GDP acceleration (GDPC1 growth > 3.0%) during identified recovery phases.
